content.full_text:
September 28, 1976


Mr. Philip G. Satre
Vargas, Bartless & Dixon
201 West Liberty St., Suite 300
Reno, Nevada 89504

Dear Mr. Satre:

Thank you for your letter of September 10, requesting information
concerning certain attempts to influence the administrative action
of the Tahoe Regional Planning Agency and whether or not that ac-
tivity would be covered under California's Political Reform Act,
Government Code Sections 82039 and 82045.

The commission's staff has determined that the Tahoe Regional
Planning Agency is not a state agency as defined in Section 82049
and, therefore, lobbying activity before the agency would not be
subject to the provisions of the Political Reform Act.

If you desire additional information, please do not hesitate to
call me.

Very truly yours,


Regina Siciliano-Kutchins
Technical Assistance Program
Coordinator


RSK:bw

bcc: Jeanne P.


RENO OFFICE
GEORGE L.VARGAS
KENNETH A.DILLON (1914-1964)
LOUIS MEAD DIXON
ROBERT W. MARSHALL
JAMES P. LOGAN
JOHN C. RENSHAW
JAMES H. CRACRAFT
FREDERICK R. STARICH
MARVIN W. MURPHY
JAMES C. BEASLEY
JOHN P. SANDE, III
PAUL D. LUCCHESI
PHILIP G. SATRE
JAMES P. LOGAN, JR.

VARGAS, BARTLETT & DIXON
ATTORNEYS AT LAW
201 WEST LIBERTY STREET, SUITE 300
P.O. OFFICE BOX 281
RENO, NEVADA 89504
(702) 786-5000

FOR CONSIDERATION
RESPONSE DUE 7-28-76

LAS VEGAS OFFICE
300 SOUTH FOURTH STREET
EGO VALLEY BANK PLAZA
LAS VEGAS, NEVADA 89101
(702) 385-4700

LOUIS MEAD DIXON
ROBERT L. GIFFORD
H. GREGORY NASKY
EDWARD H. TRICKER
WILLIAM R. EGAN
CHRIS A. BEECROFT, JR.
DEAN P. VERNON

CABLE: VARDADIX

September 10, 1976

Regina Kutchins
Technical Compliance Advisor
Fair Political Practices Commission
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804

Dear Ms. Kutchins:

In accordance with our telephone conversation of
September 7, 1976 I would like to obtain an informal
written opinion from the commission as to whether or not
the activities set forth in the following set of facts
constitute "lobbying" or the making of "payments to
influence...administrative action" of a state agency as
those terms are defined and interpreted under California
Government Code §§82039 and 82045, respectively:

A, a Nevada corporation, engages
employees and at times attorneys to
represent its interests before the Tahoe
Regional Planning Agency (created by
bi-state compact and approved by Federal
legislation) with respect to certain
rule-making and quasi-legislative
proceedings which are concerned solely with
A's Nevada interests or operations. At
times the activities of A's employees
and attorneys in this respect exceed 40
hours (including 10 hours of direct
communication) in two consecutive months
and involves employees who are engaged in
direct communication for 10% or more of
their compensated time.

It is the opinion of this office that legislation
affecting the agency can be enacted only by both states and
that Proposition 9 does not have extra-territorial
jurisdiction over what are essentially solely "Nevada"
transactions.


Regina Kutchins
September 10, 1976
Page Two


     Your cooperation has been greatly appreciated.
If you have any questions regarding this hypothetical
situation, please do not hesitate to call me collect.

                                        Yours truly,

                                        [signature]
                                        Philip G. Satre

     PGS/mgg

If you can provide a clearer version, I'll be happy to transcribe it exactly as requested.

1
2  ROBERT E. ALDERMAN JR.
3  Attorney at Law
   22210 Crenshaw Boulevard
   Torrance, California  90505
   (213) 539-2510

4

5  Attorney for plaintiffs.

6

7

8             SUPERIOR COURT FOR THE STATE OF CALIFORNIA

9                    FOR THE COUNTY OF LOS ANGELES

10

11  JAMES DAVIS, on behalf of the          )      CASE # SW C 36243
12  members of the HILLSIDE              )
    RESIDENTS ASSOCIATION,                )
13  plaintiffs                             )      POINTS AND AUTHORITIES
                                          )      IN SUPPORT OF PRELIMINARY
14         v.                             )      INJUNCTION with DECLARA-
                                          )      TIONS by MILTON ISBELL,
15  BOARD OF EDUCATION of the            )      TED PERRY, EDWIN PURNEE,
    City of Torrance, STANLEY L.         )      CAROL BYERS and MARY
16  BUDIN, OWEN H. GRIFFITH,             )      MELTON.
    NAOMI LEAVITT, HYMAN                 )
    SILVER, DOROTHY BAKER;               )
17  The advisory/administrative staff,    )      Date: May 6, 1976
    VIS. BERNARD GAREN, GEORGE           )      Time: 9:00 AM
18  CHALFESON, DR. EDWARD                )      Dept: J
    RICHARDSON, DR. LLOYD JONES;         )
19  The Torrance Unified School District, )
    and DOES I through XX inclusive,     )
20  defendants                            )
                                          )

21

22

23                           INTRODUCTION

24

25  This case is concerned with the Political Reform Act of 1974

26  which, by demand of the people, established laws requiring

27  campaign disclosures and prohibited public officials from making,

28  participating in or influencing decisions in which they had a

29  conflict of interest.

30

31  Admittedly, this is new law.  There are no Supreme Court

32  decisions to serve as guidelines.  However, it will be shown,


                                    -1-


[illegible]

that with respect to the decision of February 19, 1976 to close

the Hillside Elementary School, atleast six of the defendants had

a conflict of interest of the nature prohibited by the Political

Reform Act. Accordingly, the law requires that said decision

be declared void and that all further activity precipitating from

that decision be enjoined.

It will be shown that this new law is applicable to the school

board and the administrative staff of Torrance School District,

and that the only remedy available by law to the public is that

of injunctive relief and invalidation. The court will then be

asked to preserve the status quo by granting a preliminary

injunction prohibiting further activity toward the closing of

Hillside until the matter can be fully adjudicated on its merits.

The people of the United States, as well as the tax payers and

citizens of Torrance, are very disconcerted with the secrecy

of government, the misappropriations of funds, the power and

the "me first" attitude of politicians and the public officials.

This is demonstrated most notablly in the recent out rage at

the Watergate affair and the passing by the California citizens

of Proposition 9, the Political Reform Act.

STATEMENT OF THE FACTS:

The facts of this case are rather simple: The defendants felt

it was necessary to close an elementary school in south Torrance,

of which there are four: Hillside, Walteria, Parkway and

Riviera.

Parkway and Riviera are relatively near one another in the

southwest section, Walteria is in the south central area and

-2-


[illegible]

1    Hillside is secluded, several miles from the others, in the

2    southeast section.

3

4    Unfortunately, whenever a neighborhood elementary school is

5    closed, the residential real estate within walking distance is

6    devaluated, becomes less desirable and does not appreciate

7    in value as rapidly as similar property within walking distance

8    of an elementary school.

9

10   However, more unfortunate for the plaintiffs in this case is

11   the fact that at least six of the defendants had a financial and/or

12   personal interest in the decision as to which school should be

13   closed.  Two of them purchased homes in the new Country

14   Estates, across the street from Hillside. If Hillside remained

15   open their children would have to cross heavily trafficked Crenshaw

16   Boulevard to reach it.  Also, four of the defendants live in the

17   Parkway/Riviera district.  If they closed one of their schools,

18   they would be devaluing and reducing the desirability of their

19   own property.

20

21   Consequently, the defendants voted to close Hillside instead.

22   It is that decision, or more appropriately, the participation

23   therein by six of the most influential members of the school

24   board and the administrative staff, that the plaintiffs feel was

25   a violation of the Political Reform Act.

26

27   (Note: Although the above is an accurate summary of the facts,

28   the Court is respectfully asked to read the exhibits attached

29   hereto before proceeding with the balance of these points and

30   authorities.  By doing so, it is felt that the Court will have

31   a better grasp of what follows hereafter.)

32

-3-


1
2
3 Perhaps, but only perhaps, the same decision would have been
4 made without the participation or influence of the above defendant
5 who had a personal interest and financial interest in the out
6 come of that decision. However, the propriety or wisdom of that
7 decision is not the issue in this case.

8
9 The only issues presently are, (A) did one or more of the
10 defendants have a conflict of interest when they recommended
11 and/or voted to close the Hillside school and (B) if so, should
12 they have been disqualified from participating in that decision?

13
14 As will be shown in detail hereafter, it is not the responsibility
15 of the court at this hearing to adjudicate the ultimate issues
16 above.

17
18 The only purpose of this hearing is to issue a preliminary
19 injunction to preserve the status quo and to prevent irreparable
20 injury to the plaintiffs while they are conducting extensive and
21 thorough discovery and investigation necessary to prove the
22 allegations which they have made against the defendants.

23
24 Unless such a preliminary injunction is granted, it would be
25 futile for the plaintiffs to proceed with this action. By the
26 time the matter could be heard for the issuance of a permanent
27 injunction, the school would already be closed and it would
28 be almost impossible to re-open it. Furthermore, if the
29 preliminary injunction is not granted, it would only serve to
30 demonstrate that the Political Reform Act of 1974, demanded and
31 passed by the people, is useless and would create a lack of
32 confidence and destroy respect for the courts and government.

-4-

STATEMENT OF ISSUES;


1
2
3    DISCUSSION OF FACTS AND LAW:
4
5    A.    "NO PUBLIC OFFICIAL AT ANY LEVEL OF STATE
6    OR LOCAL GOVERNMENT SHALL MAKE, PARTICIPATE IN
7    MAKING OR IN ANY WAY ATTEMPT TO USE HIS OFFICIAL
8    POSITION TO INFLUENCE A GOVERNMENTAL DECISION IN
9    WHICH HE KNOWS OR HAS REASON TO KNOW HE HAS A FINANCIAL
10   INTEREST." (California Government Code section 87100 - emphasis
11   added.)
12
13   When the Political Reform Act, including the above section,
14   was passed by the people they had a specific purpose in mind.
15   That purpose was to make politicians and public officials more
16   controlled and responsive to the people's desires.
17
18   "The people find and declare that: (a) state and local government
19   should serve the needs and respond to the wishes of all citizens
20   equality without regard to their wealth; (b) public officials,
21   whether elected or appointed, should perform their duties in an
22   impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial
23   interests or the financial interests of persons who have supported
24   them." (Government Code section 81001 (a) and (b).
25
26   It was also intended that such reforms be stringently enforced.
27   This is indicated in 81001 (h) where it state that "previous laws
28   regulating political practices have suffered from inadequate
29   enforcement by state and local authorities." (emphasis added.)
30
31   In the present case, several of the defendants own real property
32   in sections of South Torrance that would be financially effected by
33   their decision of which school to close in South Torrance. It
34   is submitted that participation by those defendants is of the
35
36                              -8-


[illegible]

1    nsture intended to be prohibited by the Political Reform Act.
2
3    This is setforth specifically in Government Code section 87103
4    where it states: "an official has a financial interest in a decision
5    within the meaning of section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable
6    that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable
7    from its effect of the public generally, on: (a) any real property
8    in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest
9    worth more than one-thousand dollars ($1,000.00)..."
10
11   As stated previously, the law in this area is so new, that
12   there are no precedent court decisions to lend guidance.  However,
13   there is a California Fair Political Practices Commission opinion
14   dealing with facts similar to those found in this case. That
15   decision is entitled: "In the Matter of: Opinion Requested by
16   Thomas W. Oglesby, City Manager, City of Antioch;"(1 FPPC
17   opin. 71)
18
19   In the Oglesby decision, the city council of Antioch created a
20   redevelopment agency and was asked to vote on several proposals
21   regarding projects which that agency would undertake. One
22   of the city councilmen, Mel Whatley, disclosed that he had
23   ownership interests in three properties within the project area.
24   The properties consisted of his real estate office and two rental
25   properties.
26
27   The issue was whether or not either of those interests disqualified
28   councilmen Whatley from participating in the decision to grant
29   or deny the redevelopment project. The Fair Political Practices
30   Commission felt that the business entity as well as the real
31   property interest, both constituted conflict of interest of the
32   nature intended to be prohibited by the new Political Reform Act.

-6-


1
2      Whatley was thereby disqualified from participating in that
3
4      decision.
5
6          The Fair Politician Practices Commission stated at page 61 of
7
8      the Oglesby opinion that, "we are of the opinion that the decision
9
10     whether to adopt or reject the redevelopment plan is one that
11
12     will, foreseeably have a reasonably material financial effect,
13
14     distinguishable from the effect on the public generally, on the
15
16     business and real estate investment of Mr. Whatley, and therefore
17
18     is a decision in which he may not participate."
19
20
21         It is respectfully submitted that the ownership by the defendants
22
23     in the present case of real estate within areas that would be
24
25     financially effected by their decision to close Hillside rather
26
27     than Parkway or Riviera, are analogous to the Oglesby opinion
28
29     and that likewise, the participation by those defendants in the
30
31     decision to close Hillside, should be prohibited and the decision
32
33     declared void.
34
35
36         To assist the Court in making this analogy and applying California
37
38     Government Code section 87100, the Court is respectfully referred
39
40     to section 81013 wherein it is provided that the Political Reform
41
42     Act, will prevail over conflicting enactments of legislature.  Even
43
44     more important to the interpretation and application of the law
45
46     is section 81003 which specifically states that, "This title should
47
48     be liberally construed to accomplish its purposes."
49
50
51         B.    THE PROVISIONS OF THE POLITICAL REFORM ACT ARE
52
53     APPLICABLE TO THE SCHOOL BOARD AND THE ADMINISTRATIVE
54
55     STAFF.
56
57
58         As previously stated, 87106 of the Government Code, by its own
59
60
61                                    -7-

sections.analysis:
OF FACTS AND LAW:

A. "NO PUBLIC OFFICIAL AT ANY LEVEL OF STATE OR LOCAL GOVERNMENT SHALL MAKE, PARTICIPATE IN MAKING OR IN ANY WAY ATTEMPT TO USE HIS OFFICIAL POSITION TO INFLUENCE A GOVERNMENTAL DECISION IN WHICH HE KNOWS OR HAS REASON TO KNOW HE HAS A FINANCIAL INTEREST." (California Government Code section 87100 - emphasis added.)

When the Political Reform Act, including the above section, was passed by the people they had a specific purpose in mind. That purpose was to make politicians and public officials more controlled and responsive to the people's desires.

"The people find and declare that; (a) state and local government should serve the needs and respond to the wishes of all citizens equality without regard to their wealth; (b) public officials, whether elected or appointed, should perform their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the financial interests of persons who have supported them." (Government Code section 81001 (a) and (b)).

It was also intended that such reforms be stringently enforced. This is indicated in 81001 (h) where it states that "previous laws regulating political practices have suffered from inadequate enforcement by state and local authorities." (emphasis added.)

In the present case, several of the defendants own real property in sections of South Torrance that would be financially effected by their decision of which school to close in South Torrance. It is submitted that participation by those defendants is of the

The image you provided is completely white, with no visible content or text. If you have another page or section of the document that you would like me to help with, please provide it.

nature intended to be prohibited by the Political Reform Act.

This is set forth specifically in Government Code section 87103 where it states: "an official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect of the public generally, on: (a) any real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth more than one-thousand dollars ($1,000.00)..."

As stated previously, the law in this area is so new, that there are no precedent court decisions to lend guidance. However, there is a California Fair Political Practices Commission opinion dealing with facts similar to those found in this case. That decision is entitled: "In the Matter of: Opinion Requested by Thomas W. Oglesby, City Manager, City of Antioch:" (1 FPPC opin. 71)

In the Oglesby decision, the city council of Antioch created a redevelopment agency and was asked to vote on several proposals regarding projects which that agency would undertake. One of the city councilmen, Mel Whatley, disclosed that he had ownership interests in three properties within the project area. The properties consisted of his real estate office and two rental properties.

The issue was whether or not either of those interests disqualified councilmen Whatley from participating in the decision to grant or deny the redevelopment project. The Fair Political Practices Commission felt that the business entity as well as the real property interest, both constituted conflict of interest of the nature intended to be prohibited by the new Political Reform Act.

The image you provided is completely white, with no visible content or text. If you have another image or a different question, please provide it for better results.

Whatley was thereby disqualified from participating in that decision.

The Fair Political Practices Commission stated at page 81 of the Oglesby opinion that, "we are of the opinion that the decision whether to adopt or reject the redevelopment plan is one that will, foreseeably have a reasonably material financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on the public generally, on the business and real estate investment of Mr. Whatley, and therefore is a decision in which he may not participate."

It is respectfully submitted that the ownership by the defendants in the present case of real estate within areas that would be financially effected by their decision to close Hillside rather than Parkway or Riviera, are analogous to the Oglesby opinion and that likewise, the participation by those defendants in the decision to close Hillside, should be prohibited and the decision declared void.

To assist the Court in making this analogy and applying California Government Code section 87100, the Court is respectfully referred to section 81013 wherein it is provided that the Political Reform Act, will prevail over conflicting enactments of legislature. Even more important to the interpretation and application of the law is section 81003 which specifically states that, "This title should be liberally construed to accomplish its purposes."

B. THE PROVISIONS OF THE POLITICAL REFORM ACT ARE APPLICABLE TO THE SCHOOL BOARD AND THE ADMINISTRATIVE STAFF.

As previously stated, 87100 of the Government Code, by its own

Satre Vargas, Bartless & Dixon 201 West Liberty St., Suite 300 Reno, Nevada 89504 Dear Mr. Satre: Thank you for your letter of September 10, requesting information concerning certain attempts to influence the administrative action of the Tahoe Regional Planning Agency and whether or not that activity would be covered under California's Political Reform Act, Government Code Sections 82039 and 82045. The commission's staff has determined that the Tahoe Regional Planning Agency is not a state agency as defined in Section 82049 and, therefore, lobbying activity before the agency would not be subject to the provisions of the Political Reform Act. If you desire additional information, please do not hesitate to call me. Very truly yours, Regina Siciliano-Kutchins Technical Assistance Program Coordinator RSK:bw bcc: Jeanne P.
